meta.note: Every dollar reproduces rainmaker-followup/bear_inch_master.py exactly. Multiply any *_per_genAF field by a 'water generated' quantity (AF) to rescale. Unit values are sourced marginal/avoided-cost anchors, illustrative — not company figures.

benefits.label: Irrigation water
benefits.mechanism: New streamflow is diverted to alfalfa/hay. Forage is the lowest-value use per acre-foot in the basin — the central irony of the Bear.
benefits.relationship: Each diverted acre-foot of irrigation water is worth its net return to the water itself.
benefits.source: University of Nevada Reno Extension / Arizona Farm Bureau — alfalfa water use & net value per AF.

benefits.label: Municipal & industrial supply
benefits.mechanism: A small share of new streamflow is captured for cities and industry — small in volume, highest in value per acre-foot.
benefits.relationship: Each acre-foot of municipal supply is valued at the price Utah is already prepared to pay for it.
benefits.source: Bear River Development project — 220,000 AF/yr at a quoted ~$365–400/AF ($1.5–2.8B capital).

benefits.label: Hydropower
benefits.mechanism: Water is non-consumptive here — every acre-foot in the river generates as it passes the PacifiCorp dam cascade (Soda, Grace, Oneida, Cutler) on its way down.
benefits.relationship: Every acre-foot of streamflow earns run-of-river generation as it passes the cascade.
benefits.source: PacifiCorp Bear River hydroelectric cascade; ~130 kWh/AF × wholesale price.

benefits.label: Fish & recreation (water left in the river)
benefits.mechanism: The water NOT diverted stays in the channel. A fishing day is a genuinely different product from a kilowatt-hour, so recreation can stack on hydropower for this water — but never on water already sent to farms and cities.
benefits.relationship: Each acre-foot left in the channel supports angling and recreation value.
benefits.source: WestWater Research / western water markets (instream leases $115–350/AF); UF/IFAS recreation surplus ~$95/day.

benefits.label: Groundwater recharge
benefits.mechanism: Deep percolation that reaches usable aquifer storage offsets future pumping. Diffuse basin-wide percolation is mostly not recoverable, so the literature value is discounted hard.
benefits.relationship: Each acre-foot of recharge offsets future pumping at an avoided-cost value.
benefits.source: Managed-aquifer-recharge literature ($90–1,100/AF, median ~$390), discounted to $20–250/AF here.

benefits.label: Rangeland forage green-up
benefits.mechanism: Growing-season soil moisture drives forage on dryland range. Real and broad, but the marginal value of water on dryland range is far below irrigated hay.
benefits.relationship: Each acre-foot of growing-season soil moisture adds dryland forage value.
benefits.source: USU/Extension rangeland productivity; 1 AUM ≈ 780 lbs dry forage.

benefits.label: Wildfire risk reduction
benefits.mechanism: Added late-season soil moisture lowers fuel flammability. Suppression and damage costs are large, but the acre-foot → fire-probability link is genuinely unquantified, so this is carried as a bounded co-benefit, not a load-bearing number.
benefits.relationship: Each acre-foot of soil moisture is a bounded co-benefit against wildfire cost.
benefits.source: Federal suppression ~$2.9B/yr; per-acre damage ~$500; fuel-moisture link qualitative.

benefits.label: Carbon (soil + biomass)
benefits.mechanism: Incremental plant and soil carbon from green-up, valued at the EPA social cost of carbon. Most forage carbon cycles annually, so the permanent fraction is small.
benefits.relationship: Each acre-foot of green-up stores a small, permanent fraction of carbon.
benefits.source: EPA (2023/2025) social cost of carbon ~$190–255/ton CO₂ on incremental biomass.

rollups.label: Direct water value (marginal)
rollups.note: What the water itself is worth, net. The defensible economic core.

rollups.label: Ecological value (core)
rollups.note: Forage + recharge + fish/recreation — the off-river water Layer 1 ignores.

rollups.label: Combined defensible total
rollups.note: Direct water value + ecological core. These add because they price different water.

rollups.label: Economic activity supported
rollups.note: Broader downstream output with a regional multiplier. A framing, not 'value of water'.

rollups.label: Cost to develop the same water
rollups.note: Valued at the M&I price Utah already pays. A framing, not additive.

streams.label: Dust / public-health avoided cost
streams.basis: Exposed-lakebed PM2.5/arsenic; Owens Lake analog; GSL lakebed = 23–34% of Wasatch dust.

streams.label: Lake ecosystem & industry (brine shrimp, minerals, birds)
streams.basis: Minerals ~$1.1B / 5,368 jobs; brine shrimp ~$67M; GSL aggregate ~$2.5B/yr.

streams.label: Lake-effect snow / ski
streams.basis: Lake adds 5–10% of Utah snow, +5–7 ski weeks; ski ~$1.2B / 20K jobs.

gsl_terminal.honesty: This is the ONLY Great Salt Lake figure that matters for the seeding question: how much of the inch reaches the lake and what that water is jointly worth. The anchors are deliberately modest — seeding moves lake LEVEL by 2–3 orders of magnitude too little to refill it, so the defensible headline value sits upstream, not in the lake.
